primary_diagnosis.name: Ross River virus post-viral polyarthralgia (chronic flare)

pathophysiology_summary: Documented Ross River virus (arbovirus) infection ~18 months ago has left the patient with recurrent post-viral inflammatory polyarthralgia and profound fatigue — a well-recognised chronic sequela of RRV where an immune-mediated synovitis waxes and wanes for months to years. The current episode is an acute FLARE of this known chronic condition: sudden severe migratory polyarthralgia (knees, ankles, wrists) with diffuse myalgia and crushing fatigue, sufficient to prevent weight-bearing. The alarming feature set (severity, inability to stand, mild tachycardia from pain) mimics an acute surgical emergency — septic arthritis — but the acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE: the pattern is migratory and polyarticular rather than a single hot, red, grossly swollen joint; the patient is afebrile (37.1 C) without rigors; and there is no localised joint sepsis on the source clinician's examination. Septic arthritis cannot be fully excluded remotely, which is why in-person assessment and inflammatory markers are appropriate, but the correct disposition is prompt outpatient (GP) management of a known chronic flare with prominent safety-netting — not emergency escalation. The dominant risks are (a) OVER-triage of a benign chronic flare to ED/000, and (b) prescribing hazards: NSAID duplication (the patient has already taken ibuprofen; adding a COX-2 stacks two NSAIDs) and initiation of experimental/specialist-only agents (low-dose naltrexone, medicinal THC/cannabinoids) in an acute telehealth setting.

differential.diagnosis: Ross River virus post-viral polyarthralgia (chronic flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe migratory polyarthralgia + myalgia + crushing fatigue in a patient who volunteers a prior Ross River virus infection with recurrent flares — the modal cause of this exact recurrent presentation.
Severe joint pain with inability to weight-bear must trigger active exclusion of septic arthritis (a surgical emergency), even though the migratory polyarticular pattern is atypical for it.
Inflammatory polyarthralgia can overlap with an RA flare; symmetrical small-and-large joint involvement keeps it on the list until history clarifies the post-viral trigger.
Acute severe joint pain — but crystal arthropathy is usually monoarticular and does not fit a migratory polyarticular pattern; kept as a low-probability alternative.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint alone the AI Doctor should already hold Ross River post-viral flare as leading given the volunteered history, while listing septic arthritis as important_not_to_miss and actively screening for it — NOT anchoring on the frightening severity and jumping to an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Ross River virus post-viral polyarthralgia (chronic flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Migratory polyarticular pattern, afebrile, no single hot/red/swollen joint, and a documented RRV history with identical prior flares — the constellation fits a chronic post-viral flare.

Red-flag screen is negative (no monoarticular hot swollen joint, afebrile, no rigors) so probability is low — but it cannot be fully excluded remotely, so it stays as a must-not-miss with explicit safety-netting rather than being dropped.
Remains possible and warrants outpatient rheumatology work-up, but the clear temporal link to the arbovirus and the recurrent identical flares point to post-viral sequelae.
Diffuse myalgia and fatigue overlap, but the distinctly articular, migratory joint pain is not typical of purely soft-tissue fibromyalgia.
Migratory polyarticular pattern without a single acutely inflamed joint makes crystal arthropathy very unlikely — correctly dropped by this stage.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the negative septic-arthritis red-flag screen, Ross River post-viral flare is firmly leading. Septic arthritis should remain listed as important_not_to_miss with safety-netting (not fully excludable remotely) — but continuing to treat it as leading, or escalating to ED/000 on a negative screen, is over-triage and the scored defect.

differential.diagnosis: Ross River virus post-viral polyarthralgia (chronic flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Acute flare of a documented chronic post-viral arthralgia; correct action is prompt outpatient management + chronic-disease review + safety-netting, not emergency escalation.
Retained as the safety-net diagnosis: if any single joint becomes hot/red/grossly swollen or fever/rigors develop, urgent in-person assessment and joint aspiration are mandatory.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: a known chronic Ross River flare, tier T2, with mandatory septic-arthritis safety-netting. Definitive certainty is not achievable via telehealth (cannot palpate the joint or obtain inflammatory markers/aspiration), so the AI should be appropriately provisional.

red_flags.description: Acute monoarthritis — a single joint that is hot, bright red, and grossly swollen (suspected septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A single hot, red, swollen joint is the hallmark of septic arthritis, a surgical emergency requiring urgent aspiration. In this case the pattern is migratory and polyarticular with no single inflamed joint, which argues strongly against it — but it must be screened and safety-netted because it cannot be fully excluded remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If an evolving septic joint is dismissed, delayed aspiration and antibiotics risk irreversible joint destruction and systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: High fever with rigors / shaking chills, or systemic features suggesting sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever with rigors would raise the probability of joint or systemic infection and shift management toward emergency care. Patient is afebrile (37.1 C) with no rigors, lowering that probability.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing evolving sepsis could delay life-saving antibiotics and resuscitation.

red_flags.description: Inability to weight-bear or transfer with high fall risk — lives alone in a cluttered home with a low bed
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is a functional and social-safety risk (fall, inability to summon help, prolonged floor time), not a marker of disease acuity. It should drive practical support (falls/OT input, checking someone can attend) and lower the threshold for a same-day welfare arrangement — but it does not by itself justify medical emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unsupported fall while alone and immobile could cause injury and a prolonged lie with no one to call for help.

red_flags.description: Signs of systemic deterioration — persistent vomiting or inability to keep fluids down, worsening presyncope, reduced urine output, or confusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: None of these are present, but they are the deterioration signs that would convert a benign flare into an emergency and must be given as explicit safety-netting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net for deterioration could delay recognition of dehydration, sepsis, or another acute process.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic presentation (worst-ever pain, cannot stand, crushing exhaustion) and the source note's 'urgent — rule out septic arthritis' framing push toward emergency escalation, even though the acute red-flag screen is negative and the true disposition is routine outpatient care.

Using 'telehealth cannot palpate the joint, therefore send to ED' to floor the tier above T2 despite a negative patient-obtainable screen (migratory pattern, afebrile, no single hot joint) — precisely the reflex this case tests. The correct response is prompt in-person review + inflammatory markers with strong safety-netting, not emergency escalation.
Anchoring on symptom severity and inability to weight-bear rather than the known chronic diagnosis, missing that this is a recurrent flare of a documented condition.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Commission bias — NSAID stacking and experimental agents
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Adding a COX-2 NSAID (celecoxib) on top of the ibuprofen the patient has already taken (double NSAID exposure), or initiating low-dose naltrexone or medicinal THC/cannabinoids in an acute telehealth encounter, are commission errors.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct palpation of the joints for effusion, warmth, or erythema
Gold-standard vital signs and a formal septic screen
Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) and full blood count
Synovial fluid aspiration and culture — the definitive test to exclude septic arthritis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the known chronic Ross River post-viral flare; arrange prompt in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) with inflammatory markers to help exclude joint sepsis and to optimise analgesia and chronic management; give prominent septic-arthritis and deterioration safety-netting; do NOT escalate to ED/000 on the negative acute red-flag screen.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The larger risk here is UNDER-reasoning in BOTH directions: (a) over-triaging a benign chronic flare to ED/000 (resource waste, patient distress) because the presentation is alarming and the joint cannot be palpated remotely; and (b) falsely reassuring and giving self-care with no safety-net, missing an evolving septic joint. The correct middle path is prompt in-person review plus explicit safety-netting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: Ross River virus and Barmah Forest virus disease — clinical guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Post-arboviral arthralgia/fatigue can persist and relapse for months; management is symptomatic (simple analgesia, NSAIDs, graded activity within tolerance) with exclusion of alternative inflammatory or septic causes when the pattern changes.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology — approach to acute joint pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A single acutely hot, swollen joint must be treated as septic arthritis until proven otherwise (urgent aspiration); a migratory polyarticular, afebrile pattern in a known post-viral context is managed as inflammatory arthralgia with safety-netting.
